files.SKILL.md:
# Full-Surface Operations

How to carry operational work through a workspace that exposes multiple systems, sources, and audiences — and how to make each task inform the next.

## Map the surface before acting

At task start, inventory every system the environment exposes and read their current state in parallel. The loudest item does not define the scope of the task — the task surface is everything available. Missing a channel, a tracker, or a scheduling entry is the most common source of silent failure.

Stop reading when you have enough to act on the most urgent item, not before.

## Triage by stakes

Critical issues and material risks first. Time-sensitive work second. Routine items third. Handle the highest-stakes item completely — including any follow-through it requires — before returning to the queue. Do not let low-stakes items pull attention away from a material one.

## Ground every value in observation

Every name, identifier, time, number, and quoted fact in your output traces to data you read in this session. Copy verbatim — no paraphrase, no plausible-looking stand-in. If a required value is missing, say so explicitly rather than inventing one. Silent wrong values against the wrong record are the worst class of error.

## Name specific owners, not channels

Directed work is a named person paired with a named next action. A post to a channel broadcasts; it does not assign. When work needs to land on someone, tag them directly with the specific ask. Broadcasts are for awareness only — always in addition to a directly tagged owner, never in place of it.

## Tier detail to the audience

- **Operational peers.** Working detail, internal references, current hypothesis. Enough for them to pick up the thread.
- **Leadership or oversight.** State, risk, what is at stake, next checkpoint. Light on mechanics.
- **External counterparts.** Impact and next steps only. Strip internal system names, identifiers, unreleased plans, and any topic they would not already know from their own world.

Proper-noun test for external or broadcast drafts: if the recipient did not know the term independently of your workspace, strip it or replace with a generic phrase.

## Commit in concrete time

"By 15:00 today" and "inside the next hour" are commitments. "Soon", "shortly", "ASAP", and "later today" are not — they erode trust faster than missed deadlines. When the timing is not yet known, commit instead to the moment you expect to know.

## Verify substantive changes

After a write, re-read the resource itself. A response that does not clearly reflect the new state is ambiguous; the resource state is not. One extra read catches a whole class of silent failures that no retry strategy recovers from.

## Capture follow-through as artifacts

Every piece of work that extends past the current action becomes its own artifact: one per (verb × owner). Investigate, communicate, review, schedule, decide — each verb on each owner is its own trackable item. Do not bundle.

Populate participants and next-step specifics from observed data. A stranger picking up the artifact later should be able to act without asking you a question first.

## Handle ambiguity by naming it

When the right move is unclear, surface the specific signal that would resolve it. Escalate on the ambiguity rather than guessing, and rather than stalling. A short, explicit "I need X to decide Y by time Z" is worth more than a long hedged draft.

## Memory across tasks

Your persistent notes carry across this session's work. Treat them as a working library, not a diary — their value is what they teach you on the next task, not what they record about the last one.

- **Extract shape, not surface.** Log the underlying pattern — the process, the trap, the signal that worked — not the specifics. Only structure transfers across tasks; names, identifiers, and timestamps rotate per task and mislead if carried forward.
- **Log mistakes with root causes.** A recorded mistake prevents repetition only if the root cause is explicit. "This failed because X" beats "avoid this". Consult before similar work.
- **Stamp; supersede rather than delete; prefer recent.** When a later observation contradicts an earlier note, mark the earlier one superseded and keep it — the contradiction itself is evidence the fact moves. On retrieval conflict, older notes are hints, not authority.
- **Read notes once per task.** Consult at the start, carry the relevant shape forward in working memory, and do not re-read mid-task except to resolve a specific named question.
- **Persist after acting.** When a task is complete, add to the library any pattern, mistake, or superseded fact worth carrying forward. Skip this and the next task starts cold.
